metadata.split_role: Core/restored baseline mushroom records kept at the original path.
metadata.notes: Core/restored mushroom records retained at the original mushrooms.json path. Expanded mushroom records now load from smaller grouped files listed in split_manifest. v4.2.80 adds a core mushroom credibility pass: source links, use roles, foraging values, card notes, and cleaned placeholder use text for high-visibility mushrooms. v4.2.99-r2026-05-11-fold-mushroom-photo-patches1: folded mushroom photo patch records directly into canonical source records and retired runtime PHOTO_PATCH_PATHS. v4.3.0-r2026-05-11-data-chunk-size-cleanup1 split active data sources by size; record bodies and source order preserved. v4.3.61-r2026-05-14-mushroom-completion-wave1-15species: completed selected mushroom species records in-place with rights metadata and stale review flags cleared.

split_manifest.role: Core/restored baseline mushroom records kept at the original path.
Expanded porcini, Tylopilus, dark-pored, and brown bolete records.
Expanded red/yellow, blue-staining, red-pored, and caution-lane bolete records.
Expanded Suillus and Leccinum/scaber-stalk bolete records.
Expanded long-stemmed, peppery, gilled-bolete, wood-inhabiting, and oddball bolete records.

review_reason_audit.rule: Normalize explicit review signals into review_reasons/review_note and prefix notes for Needs Review list visibility.

metadata.split_note: v4.3.0-r2026-05-11-data-chunk-size-cleanup1 split this active data source by file size to keep working chunks under roughly 75–100 KB. Record bodies and source order were preserved.
metadata.last_reaudit_note: Re-audited v4.3.61-v4.3.63 touched mushroom species against hard completion checklist; incomplete items explicitly declared instead of silently marked complete.

records.notes: Cap hangs partly free from the stem rather than being attached all the way down.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Cook like other true morels after cleaning and lengthwise confirmation. Do not eat raw.
records.general_notes: Half-free morels are real morels, but their cap attachment makes beginners nervous. The hollow interior and true pitted cap are the key checks.
records.edibility_detail: Good edible after confident true-morel identification. Slice lengthwise to confirm hollow interior and cap attachment; avoid any brainy, chambered, or cottony false morels.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Spring morel season, usually May in Upper Michigan.

records.photo_patch_group: Batch A — morels and early morel relatives
records.field_identification: Spring morel with a cap attached only partway down the stem, leaving the lower cap edge hanging free like a skirt. Cap has true pits and ridges, and the mushroom is hollow from cap tip through stem when sliced lengthwise.
records.mushroom_card_note: Spring true morel with a skirt-like half-free cap; slice lengthwise before trusting it.

use_links.label: MSU Extension: May is Morel Month
use_links.notes: Michigan-oriented morel safety and harvest context.

edible_use.notes: Good edible after confident true-morel identification. Slice lengthwise to confirm hollow interior and cap attachment; avoid any brainy, chambered, or cottony false morels.

completion_audit.auditor_note: Species touched in v4.3.61-v4.3.63 was rechecked against the hard completion checklist. A record is only marked complete when all checklist items pass; otherwise the missing requirement is explicitly declared.

records.notes: Instead of gills or pores, these have short dangling teeth under the cap.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Trim dirty tooth layer if needed. Good sautéed or added to mixed mushroom dishes; texture stays firmer than many gilled mushrooms.
records.general_notes: One of the friendlier edible mushrooms for beginners because true teeth are easier to read than subtle gill attachment.
records.edibility_detail: Choice edible when young and confidently identified by the tooth underside. Older specimens can become bitter or buggy.

records.photo_patch_group: Batch C — gilled/ridged edibles and caution-adjacent species
records.field_identification: Pale buff to orange-tan cap with blunt, removable teeth under the cap instead of gills or pores. Teeth run slightly down the stem; flesh is firm and brittle. The tooth underside is the deal-breaker ID feature.
records.mushroom_card_note: Look underneath: blunt hanging teeth, not gills. Choice edible when young.

edible_use.notes: Choice edible when young and confidently identified by the tooth underside. Older specimens can become bitter or buggy.

completion_audit.auditor_note: Species touched in v4.3.61-v4.3.63 was rechecked against the hard completion checklist. A record is only marked complete when all checklist items pass; otherwise the missing requirement is explicitly declared.

records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional reishi-style use; app should present this as traditional/field-guide use rather than medical treatment.
records.culinary_uses: Not eaten as a mushroom. Usually dried for tea/tincture by people who already know what they are doing.
records.edibility_detail: Not a food mushroom. Used in some traditional/reishi-style preparations, but this guide treats it as a cautious medicinal/practical ID entry, not a meal.
records.general_notes: Host matters: Ganoderma tsugae is strongly associated with hemlock/conifer wood, separating it from some hardwood Ganoderma look-alikes.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Annual bracket; most noticeable summer into fall.

records.photo_patch_basis: exact slug match in uploaded mushrooms.json
records.photo_patch_group: Batch E — conks, chanterelle allies, and caution gilled record
records.field_identification: Glossy varnished red to reddish-brown shelf or kidney-shaped bracket on hemlock or other conifer wood. White pore surface bruises brown; flesh is corky/woody, not a tender eating mushroom.
records.mushroom_card_note: Glossy red varnished shelf on hemlock; not food, traditional-use only.

edible_use.notes: Not a food mushroom. Used in some traditional/reishi-style preparations, but this guide treats it as a cautious medicinal/practical ID entry, not a meal.

completion_audit.auditor_note: Species touched in v4.3.61-v4.3.63 was rechecked against the hard completion checklist. A record is only marked complete when all checklist items pass; otherwise the missing requirement is explicitly declared.

records.notes: Large clustered rosette of overlapping fronds at the base of oaks and other hardwoods.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Maitake has supplement interest, but this guide treats wild hens mainly as a prime food mushroom.
records.culinary_uses: Choice edible. Trim away tough bases, clean the layered fronds well, and cook; useful sautéed, roasted, dried, or in soups and gravies.
records.general_notes: A good host-linked species and one of the better fall edible clusters.
records.edibility_detail: Choice edible when fresh, young, and confidently identified; older rosettes get tough or bug-ridden.

staining: Blackens when old or bruised slightly
mushroom_profile.season_note: Mostly fall, especially at oak bases.

records.field_identification: Large gray-brown rosette of many overlapping spoon- or fan-shaped fronds, usually at the base of oak or other hardwoods. The underside has pores, not gills, and the fruitbody arises from a branching base.
records.mushroom_card_note: Choice fall edible at oak bases; look for a big layered rosette with pored undersides.

records.notes: Clustered gilled mushrooms on wood and roots.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Experienced-forager only; never raw, never old, and never without a confident white spore print and wood/root association. This app keeps them in the caution lane.
records.non_edible_severity: Caution — toxic look-alikes and individual reactions
records.edibility_detail: Caution entry. Some honey mushrooms are eaten by experienced foragers, but reactions are common and confusion with dangerous gilled mushrooms is possible. This guide does not treat them as beginner food.
records.general_notes: Honey mushrooms are a species complex. The combination of clustered growth from wood/roots, white spores, ring/rhizomorph clues, and lack of orange glow helps separate them from jack-o-lanterns.

records.photo_patch_group: Batch C — gilled/ridged edibles and caution-adjacent species
records.field_identification: Dense clusters on wood, buried roots, or at tree bases; tan to honey-brown caps often with small scales; white spore print; attached to slightly decurrent pale gills; many have a ring on the stem and dark shoestring-like rhizomorphs near the wood.
records.mushroom_card_note: Clustered wood/root mushroom with white spores; caution lane, not beginner food.

edible_use.notes: Caution entry. Some honey mushrooms are eaten by experienced foragers, but reactions are common and confusion with dangerous gilled mushrooms is possible. This guide does not treat them as beginner food.

completion_audit.auditor_note: Species touched in v4.3.61-v4.3.63 was rechecked against the hard completion checklist. A record is only marked complete when all checklist items pass; otherwise the missing requirement is explicitly declared.

records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.culinary_uses: Do not eat. Included as a poisonous chanterelle look-alike caution record.
records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food in this guide; keep as an identification/caution record.
records.general_notes: This should stay in the caution side of the app. It is a key chanterelle look-alike and belongs in the gilled lane, not 'other'.

records.poisoning_effects: Poisonous mushroom associated with gastrointestinal illness. Do not eat; the main app value is comparison against chanterelles and other orange mushrooms.
records.field_identification: Bright orange to pumpkin-orange mushroom, usually in dense clusters from stumps, dead hardwood, or buried roots; true sharp gills run down the stem; flesh is orange to orangish when cut; spore print is white to pale yellow.
records.overview: Poisonous orange wood-growing mushroom often confused with chanterelles when it appears to fruit from buried wood.
records.look_alike_notes: Chanterelles have blunt ridges rather than true gills and usually do not grow in dense clusters from wood. Jack-o'-lantern has true decurrent gills and a wood/buried-root connection.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Omphalotus illudens
use_links.notes: Clustered wood growth, orange color, true gills, and chanterelle comparison.

records.mushroom_card_note: Poisonous chanterelle look-alike: orange clustered wood-grower with true gills.
image_review_reasons: Photo rights metadata verified and completed in v4.3.61-r2026-05-14-mushroom-completion-wave1-15species.

records.notes: King Bolete / porcini is kept as a practical field-guide page, but North American Boletus edulis-like mushrooms are taxonomically messy. Treat this as an edulis-group/porcini-style entry unless a collection is confirmed to species level.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.culinary_uses: Excellent sautéed, dried, or used in soups and sauces when young and firm. Avoid soft, old, bug-ridden specimens.
records.general_notes: Use as a group record for porcini/king bolete types in the region; do not treat every brown-capped bolete as king bolete.
reviewReasons: Re-audit incomplete: photo coverage is below the 2-image minimum using exact-rights images.
records.edibility_detail: Choice edible for experienced bolete foragers after confirming white-to-yellow pores, no blue staining, no red pores, and no bitter taste clue. North American taxonomy is messy, so treat this as the Boletus edulis group.
review_reasons: Re-audit incomplete: photo coverage is below the 2-image minimum using exact-rights images.
records.review_note: Needs review: regional/taxonomy confirmation for the North American Boletus edulis group.
records.review_notes: Needs review: regional/taxonomy confirmation for the North American Boletus edulis group.
records.duplicate_note: Duplicate King Bolete record kept only as a legacy slug alias.

records.foraging_value: Prime foraging — species group needs care
records.mushroom_card_note: Porcini lane: white flesh, no blue staining, no red pores, reticulate upper stem.
records.field_identification: Stout bolete with a brown bun-like cap, white pores when young becoming yellow-olive with age, thick pale stem often with fine white netting near the top, and firm white flesh that does not blue-stain.

edible_use.notes: Choice edible for experienced bolete foragers after confirming white-to-yellow pores, no blue staining, no red pores, and no bitter taste clue. North American taxonomy is messy, so treat this as the Boletus edulis group.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Usually encountered from September through November in the Upper Midwest / Great Lakes window.

records.notes: Larch Suillus is treated here as Suillus clintonianus, the North American larch/tamarack-associated species often shown in older guides as Suillus grevillei. Check for larch/tamarack nearby.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily culinary/ID interest in this guide.
records.culinary_uses: Edible when young and carefully prepared, but sticky cap skin and soft texture make it less desirable than prime boletes. Best cooked; peel slimy cap skin if objectionable.
records.edibility_detail: Treat as caution / not for meals in this guide until species-level edibility is clarified.

mushroom_profile.summary: Minimal bolete seed entry for Larch Suillus. Added in the Michigan / Upper Midwest bolete pass; fuller ID notes, sources, and images still need review.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Usually encountered from July through September in the Upper Midwest / Great Lakes window.
research_notes: Species seed added from the Michigan / Upper Midwest bolete pass.
Needs source links and fuller field notes.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Suillus clintonianus
use_links.notes: North American larch/tamarack Suillus; separated from European Suillus grevillei.

records.field_identification: Sticky orange-yellow to reddish Suillus associated with larch/tamarack; check for nearby larch and a slimy cap.
records.mushroom_card_note: Fair Suillus tied to larch/tamarack; edible but not a prime bolete.

records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Popular medicinal-interest species, especially for nerve/cognition claims, but this app should treat claims as preliminary/traditional unless supported by specific research.
records.culinary_uses: Tear or slice fresh white flesh and cook thoroughly; often used like seafood-textured mushroom in sautés or patties.
records.general_notes: Separate from bear’s head tooth and coral tooth by growth form: lion’s mane is mostly unbranched with one compact mass of teeth.
reviewReasons: Re-audit incomplete: photo coverage is below the 2-image minimum using exact-rights images.
records.edibility_detail: Choice edible when fresh, white, and confidently identified. Avoid yellowed, soggy, or old specimens.
records.field_identification: Single unbranched white pom-pom or beard of long dangling teeth, usually from a wound on living hardwood or from recently dead hardwood. It lacks the branched coral-like structure of other Hericium species.
records.mushroom_card_note: White unbranched pom-pom of dangling teeth on hardwood; choice food, medicinal-interest claims stay cautious.

records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Cook young, clean specimens only after confident ID. Discard anything with chemical/phenolic smell, strong yellow staining, or uncertain base.
records.edibility_detail: Edible only after careful Agaricus identification. Beginners should avoid any white mushroom until they can rule out Amanita: check gill color, spore print, stem base, ring, bruising, and odor.
records.general_notes: This overlaps the field mushroom idea; keep the deadly Amanita contrast visible because white lawn mushrooms are a common mistake zone.

staining: May bruise slightly pinkish; avoid strong yellow staining

records.photo_patch_group: Batch C — gilled/ridged edibles and caution-adjacent species
records.field_identification: Grassland Agaricus with pink young gills that turn chocolate brown, free gills, no volva/cup at the base, and a brown spore print. It should not have white mature gills, a bulbous cup, or a chemical yellow-staining smell.
records.mushroom_card_note: Pink-to-brown gills and brown spore print are required; white gills/cup at base = stop.

edible_use.notes: Edible only after careful Agaricus identification. Beginners should avoid any white mushroom until they can rule out Amanita: check gill color, spore print, stem base, ring, bruising, and odor.
